affordances.name: Adversarial failure chain map
affordances.mechanism: When a plan depends on security, adversarial response, or control reliability, require the answer to name attacker or misaligned-stakeholder incentives, asset specificity, attack-surface reality, operational ownership, and cascading failure chains.

use_when: The problem is adversarial, fast-changing, and can cascade across technical, human, and governance layers.
Defenders must model attacker incentives, failure chains, and cross-domain consequences rather than just enumerate controls.
Security decisions need pressure-testing against realistic scenarios instead of static compliance or architecture diagrams alone.
do_not_use_when: The team is fetishizing threat models, simulations, or mental frameworks while skipping asset specificity, attack surface reality, or operational ownership.
Scenario work creates false certainty about rare threats while everyday control failures, shortcuts, or handoff gaps remain unexamined.
The plan assumes all actors touching execution are aligned with the measured outcome.
case_evidence_needed: The asset, dependency, or surface being protected.
The adversary or misaligned stakeholder and their incentive.
The control owner and operational handoff.
The plausible failure chain and fallback gap.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to identify the asset, adversary or misaligned actor, attack or failure chain, control owner, and fallback dependency before treating the security plan as robust.
evidence_required: The control owner and handoff path.
The cascading failure path or fallback gap.
good_output_shape: Names the adversarial or misaligned actor.
Links asset specificity to attack surface and ownership.
Describes the failure chain rather than listing controls.
Shows where the plan needs a fallback or escalation path.

diagnostic_questions: Who benefits if this plan underperforms quietly rather than fails visibly?
Which asset, attack surface, or dependency is being protected by whom?
What failure chain crosses technical, human, or governance layers?
misuse_guards: Do not treat control enumeration as security.
Do not create false certainty with simulations that skip everyday handoff gaps.
Do not assume every actor touching execution is aligned with the outcome being measured.

source_evidence.source_quote: the problem is adversarial, fast-changing, and capable of cascading across technical, human, and governance layers at once
model attacker incentives, failure chains, and cross-domain consequences rather than just enumerate controls
security decisions must be pressure-tested against realistic scenarios instead of static compliance or architecture diagrams alone

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 9 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports one adversarial failure-chain affordance for assets, incentives, control ownership, and cascading failures.
dropped_material: Did not extract generic mental-model-lattice or AI digital twin material as separate security affordances.
Kept FUD and game-theory material only as supporting evidence for adversarial pressure testing.
open_questions: PR43 should test whether this card improves packet handoff without making security advice a checklist of controls.

absence_records.reason: The source says defenders must model incentives, failure chains, and cross-domain consequences rather than just enumerate controls.
The source warns that threat models stay superficial when the plan assumes every stakeholder touching execution is trying to produce the same measured outcome.

source_evidence.source_quote: Threat models stay superficial when the plan assumes every stakeholder who touches execution is trying to produce the same outcome the decision-maker wants.
complex problem-solving in security requires anticipation, adversarial analysis, and systemic understanding

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 9 source reading found strong reviewed depth for cybersecurity-thinking-models as adversarial failure-chain mapping, not generic security control listing.
dropped_material: No runtime, prompt, lane, packet, or user-facing change is implied.
open_questions: Later packet review should test whether this card catches misaligned actors and ownership gaps without overclaiming full cybersecurity doctrine.
